howItWorks.intro: Your one-rep max (1RM) is the maximum weight you can lift for a single repetition. This calculator estimates it from a lighter set using five validated strength formulas.
rules: Perform a set of 2-12 reps to failure (or near failure) with good form
Enter the weight and reps completed — the calculator applies 5 formulas
The average of all formulas gives the most reliable estimate
howItWorks.outro: These formulas are most accurate in the 2-10 rep range. Above 10 reps, accuracy decreases. Never attempt an actual 1RM without a spotter and proper warm-up.

faq.question: How accurate are 1RM calculators?
faq.answer: 1RM calculators are generally accurate within 5-10% for the 2-6 rep range. Accuracy decreases with higher rep counts. The formulas work best for compound lifts like bench press, squat, and deadlift. Individual variation in muscle fiber type can affect results.

faq.question: Which formula is the most accurate?
faq.answer: No single formula is universally most accurate — it varies by individual and exercise. The Brzycki formula is popular for lower rep ranges (2-5), while Epley works well across a broader range. That's why this calculator averages multiple formulas for the best estimate.

faq.question: Why should I know my one-rep max?
faq.answer: Knowing your 1RM helps you program training percentages. Many strength programs prescribe weights as a percentage of your 1RM (e.g., 5 sets of 5 at 75%). It also provides a benchmark to track strength progress over time.

faq.question: Is it safe to test my actual 1RM?
faq.answer: Testing a true 1RM carries higher injury risk than normal training. If you do test it, warm up thoroughly, use a spotter, ensure proper form, and only attempt it when fully recovered. Using a calculator from a 3-5 rep set is safer and nearly as accurate.

faq.question: How often should I recalculate my 1RM?
faq.answer: Recalculate every 4-8 weeks or whenever you complete a training cycle. Strength gains come gradually, so more frequent testing isn't necessary. Simply repeat a set to failure with a familiar weight and plug in the new numbers.

faq.question: Why do different formulas give different results?
faq.answer: Each formula was developed using different study populations and statistical methods. Epley uses a linear relationship, Brzycki uses a slightly different curve, and others account for fatigue differently. The variation is usually small (within 5%), which is why averaging works well.

sections.title: What Is the One Rep Max Calculator?
sections.body: The One Rep Max Calculator is a free online tool designed for athletes and fitness enthusiasts who need quick, accurate calculations in the fitness and training space. By entering your unit, weight lifted, reps performed, you get instant results including estimated 1rm, epley, brzycki. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting estimated 1rm right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In fitness and training, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by athletes and fitness enthusiasts worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to optimize your training with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your unit and need to find the right estimated 1rm. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

howToSteps.description: Type or select your weight lifted in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Enter Your Reps Performed (2-12)
howToSteps.description: Type or select your reps performed in the field provided. Use the most accurate value available for best results.

howToSteps.title: Click Calculate
howToSteps.description: Hit the Calculate button to run the numbers. Results appear instantly below.

howToSteps.title: Review Your Results
howToSteps.description: Check your estimated 1rm, epley, brzycki. Use these figures to inform your next decision or compare against alternative scenarios.

tips: Double-check your unit before calculating — even small input errors can significantly change your results.
Run the calculator with different values to compare scenarios and find the optimal approach for your situation.
Pay attention to both estimated 1rm and epley — they work together to give you the full picture.
Bookmark this page for quick access next time you need to optimize your training.
If you're unsure about your reps performed, start with a conservative estimate and adjust from there.
